statement.markdown: Given an adjacency list representation of a graph, implement a function to detect if the graph contains a cycle. You can use either DFS or BFS.
statement.inputFormat: Input will be an adjacency list, where each element is a node with its neighbors.
statement.outputFormat: Return True if the graph contains a cycle, False otherwise.
statement.notes: The graph may contain multiple connected components.

examples.explanation: No cycle exists in this graph

hints.text: Think about how you can traverse the graph to detect a cycle

editorial.title: Detecting Cycles in a Graph: Editorial
editorial.intuition: Use a visited array to keep track of visited nodes while traversing the graph.
editorial.bruteForce: You can use a simple recursive DFS to visit all nodes.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a visited array and mark nodes as 'visiting' or 'visited' to detect cycles.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This solution works because we mark nodes as 'visiting' before visiting their neighbors.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run with sample input

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(bool visited[], int adj[], int n); };
starterCode.java: public boolean solve(boolean[] visited, int[] adj, int n) { // TODO: implement };
starterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, visited: list[bool], adj: list[int], n: int) -> bool: pass
starterCode.javascript: var solve = function(visited, adj, n) { // TODO: implement };

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(bool visited[], int adj[], int n) { for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) if (!visited[i]) { visited[i] = true; if (solve(visited, adj, i)) return true; } return false; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public boolean solve(boolean[] visited, int[] adj, int n) { for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) if (!visited[i]) { visited[i] = true; if (solve(visited, adj, i)) return true; } return false; }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, visited: list[bool], adj: list[int], n: int) -> bool: for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: visited[i] = True; if self.solve(visited, adj, i): return True return False
buggyStarterCode.javascript: var solve = function(visited, adj, n) { for (var i = 0; i < n; i++) if (!visited[i]) { visited[i] = true; if (solve(visited, adj, i)) return true; } return false; };
